paragraphs: The Bush lemon is the naturalised rough lemon (Citrus jambhiri), a vigorous citrus hybrid of mandarin and citron that escaped cultivation and grows wild across subtropical Australia. Hardy, drought resistant and cold tolerant, it adapted to tough conditions and now grows feral, which is how it earned the bush lemon name. It forms a large, often thorny tree.

Its fruit has a thick, rough, knobbly rind and is seedy, making it mostly rind and less suited to eating than selected lemons like Eureka or Lisbon. Its main historical value is as a rootstock, valued for vigour and drought tolerance, onto which finer lemon and citrus cultivars are budded. The fruit is still used at home for juice and zest.

claims.text: Bush lemon is the rough lemon, Citrus jambhiri, a cross of mandarin orange and citron
Naturalised and growing wild (feral) across subtropical Australia
Hardy, drought resistant and cold-hardy, forming a large tree
Fruit is mostly rough rind and seedy, less edible than selected lemons
Mainly used as a citrus rootstock, valued for drought tolerance

paragraphs: Eureka originated in Los Angeles, California, selected from seedlings grown from Italian seed and introduced around 1877 as Garey's Eureka. Along with Lisbon it became one of the world's principal lemon varieties.

The tree is largely thornless and strongly everbearing, producing fruit at the ends of long branches, which makes it a popular choice for home gardens. The acidic fruit has a fairly rough, prominently ridged rind and only a small nipple.

claims.text: Originated in Los Angeles, California, from seedlings of Italian seed and introduced around 1877 as Garey's Eureka
Along with Lisbon, became one of the world's principal lemon varieties
Tree is largely thornless and strongly everbearing, fruiting at the ends of long branches
Acidic fruit with a fairly rough, prominently ridged rind and a small nipple

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Frost nucellar Eureka lemon

paragraphs: Fino is a leading Spanish commercial lemon, the most widely grown cultivar in Spain, probably arising as a seedling of a common Murcia-region lemon. It is an early to mid-season (mainly winter) lemon. The tree is exceptionally vigorous, large, very thorny and highly productive, with dense foliage.

Compared with the other major Spanish lemon, Berna (Verna), Fino fruit is paler, with a smoother and thinner rind, and is higher in juice and acid content, though more seedy. The fruit is spherical to oval with a small, sharp-pointed nipple. Its high juice yield and acidity make it a favourite of processors.

claims.text: Fino probably originated as a seedling of a common Murcia-region lemon and is a major Spanish cultivar
Crop comes mainly in winter (early to mid season)
Tree is exceptionally vigorous, large, very thorny and highly productive
Fruit is paler with smoother, thinner rind and higher juice and acid content than Berna, but more seedy
Fruit is spherical to oval with a small, sharp-pointed nipple

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UCR: Limonero Fino 49 lemon
Frutas y Hortalizas: Lemon types and varieties

paragraphs: Lemonade is not a true lemon but a sweet, low-acid hybrid widely believed to be a cross of lemon and mandarin, discovered as a chance seedling in New Zealand in the early 1980s and now grown across Australia. Its exact parentage remains uncertain. The low acidity lets the natural sugars come through, giving a mild, sweet, faintly tangy flavour quite unlike a sour lemon.

Unlike a cooking lemon, ripe Lemonade fruit can be peeled and eaten fresh straight off the tree, segmenting easily thanks to its mandarin heritage. The fruit has a glossy yellow rind and juicy yellow flesh, sweetens the longer it hangs on the tree, and is also juiced for a naturally sweet drink. The tree is heavy bearing and often thorny.

claims.text: Sweet, low-acid hybrid generally regarded as a lemon by mandarin cross
Discovered as a chance seedling in New Zealand in the early 1980s
Low acidity gives a sweet, mild flavour rather than sour
Can be peeled and eaten fresh off the tree, segmenting like a mandarin
Tree is heavy bearing and often thorny

paragraphs: Lisbon is a lemon of Portuguese origin, believed to be a Gallego seedling selection, and was introduced to Australia early in the 1800s. It became one of the leading commercial lemons in both Australia and the United States.

The tree is vigorous, large, thorny and densely foliated, and is notably resistant to frost, heat, wind and neglect, more so than Eureka. Because much of the fruit is borne within the canopy it is well protected, and the oblong, very acidic fruit carries a prominent nipple.

claims.text: Lemon of Portuguese origin, believed to be a Gallego seedling selection, introduced to Australia early in the 1800s
Became a leading commercial lemon in Australia and the United States
Tree is vigorous, large, thorny and densely foliated, and notably resistant to frost, heat, wind and neglect, more so than Eureka
Much of the fruit is borne within the canopy, providing protection
Oblong, very acidic fruit with a prominent nipple

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Frost nucellar Lisbon lemon

paragraphs: The Meyer lemon is native to China and was introduced to the United States in 1908 by USDA plant explorer Frank Meyer. It is not a true lemon but a hybrid, generally described as a cross of citron with a mandarin/pomelo type.

Its fruit is sweeter and less acidic than standard Eureka or Lisbon lemons, with thin, deep-yellow to faintly orange skin and a subtle floral, almost orange-like character. A virus-free selection was released by the University of California in 1975 as the Improved Meyer lemon.

claims.text: Native to China and introduced to the United States in 1908 by USDA plant explorer Frank Meyer
Not a true lemon but a hybrid, generally a cross of citron with a mandarin/pomelo type
Sweeter and less acidic than Eureka or Lisbon lemons, with a subtle orange-like character
Thin, deep-yellow to faintly orange skin
A virus-free selection was released by the University of California in 1975 as the Improved Meyer lemon

paragraphs: Seedless Eureka is a selection of the classic Eureka lemon that arose as a limb sport (bud mutation) and is distinguished from ordinary Eureka by its consistently seedless fruit. In other respects it closely resembles its parent, carrying the same bright, tart, high-acid lemon flavour collectors expect from a Eureka.

The fruit is medium to large with smooth, thin yellow skin that zests and slices cleanly, and like the Eureka the tree tends to be near everbearing, cropping over much of the year rather than in one short flush. The seedlessness makes it convenient for juicing and cooking without picking out pips.

claims.text: Seedless Eureka is a selection of Eureka that arose as a limb sport (bud mutation)
Distinguished from ordinary Eureka by consistently seedless fruit, otherwise similar
Retains the classic tart, high-acid Eureka lemon flavour
Eureka type tends to be near everbearing, cropping over much of the year

sources.name: St Clements Citrus: Discovering the Lemon Seedless Eureka
Victorian Citrus Farms: Eureka Seedless lemons

paragraphs: The Variegated Eureka, also called the variegated pink lemon or pink-fleshed Eureka, is an ornamental sport that appeared on an ordinary Eureka lemon tree in Burbank, California, in 1931. It is prized as much for its looks as its fruit, with green-and-cream variegated foliage and immature fruit that is striped green and yellow before ripening to yellow.

Inside, the flesh is a soft pink (similar in look to a pink grapefruit), though the juice runs clear rather than pink. The flavour is a true acidic lemon, useful in the kitchen wherever a standard Eureka would be, so the variety offers the novelty of pink flesh and striped, variegated growth without losing classic lemon tartness.

claims.text: Variegated Eureka is the variegated pink lemon, a sport on a Eureka tree in Burbank, California, in 1931
Has green-and-cream variegated foliage and green-striped rind before ripening
Flesh is pink while the juice is clear rather than pink
Flavour is a true acidic lemon, used like a standard Eureka

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UCR: Variegated Pink Fleshed Eureka lemon
Gardenia.net: Citrus x limon Eureka Pink Variegated

paragraphs: Verna (also spelt Berna) is a major Spanish lemon of unknown origin, historically the dominant lemon of the Spanish Levant and still the second most grown Spanish cultivar after Fino. It is a distinctly late-season variety, ripening from late winter into summer and holding well on the tree, which extends the supply beyond earlier lemons.

The fruit is medium sized, oval to broad-elliptical, with a short neck, a well developed nipple and bright yellow rind at maturity. The medium-thick rind is somewhat pebbled and rough, and seed content is variable but usually few to none. It is grown primarily as a fresh-market table lemon.

claims.text: Verna is also spelt Berna, a major Spanish lemon of unknown origin
Historically the most important Spanish variety in the Levant, now second to Fino
Late-season variety ripening from late winter into summer and holding well
Fruit medium, oval to broad-elliptical, bright yellow, with somewhat rough medium-thick rind
Seed content variable but usually few to none

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection at UCR: Berna lemon
Frutas y Hortalizas: Lemon types and varieties

paragraphs: Yuzu (Citrus junos) is a distinct cold-hardy citrus, not a true lemon. It is an F1 hybrid of a mandarin and the ichang papeda, originating in central China and long cultivated in Japan and Korea. Its papeda ancestry makes it unusually frost tolerant, growing where more sensitive citrus would fail (winters down to around minus 7 degrees Celsius). The fruit resembles a small, bumpy yellow to green mandarin with uneven skin.

Yuzu is prized for its intensely aromatic rind and tart, seedy juice rather than for fresh eating, which it is rarely used for. The zest and juice carry a complex fragrance blending lemon, mandarin and grapefruit with herbal notes, and feature heavily in Japanese and Korean cooking, including the citrus sauce ponzu and the chilli paste yuzu kosho.

claims.text: Yuzu is Citrus junos, an F1 hybrid of a mandarin and the ichang papeda
Originated in central China and is cultivated in Japan and Korea
One of the most cold-hardy citrus, tolerating winters to about minus 7 degrees Celsius
Valued for aromatic rind and tart juice, rarely eaten fresh
Used in ponzu and yuzu kosho in Japanese and Korean cooking
